parentQuestion: Which receptor type is found at ALL autonomic ganglia?

scaffolds.q: What are the two main types of acetylcholine (cholinergic) receptors?
scaffolds.explain: ACh receptors come in two types: NICOTINIC (named for nicotine) and MUSCARINIC (named for muscarine, a mushroom toxin). Both bind ACh but work very differently.

wrongExplains.0: Alpha and beta are types of ADRENERGIC receptors that bind norepinephrine, not ACh.
wrongExplains.2: While they do differ in speed, the classification is nicotinic vs muscarinic.
wrongExplains.3: Both types can produce different effects. The classification is based on which drug activates them.

scaffolds.q: Nicotinic receptors are what type of receptor?
scaffolds.explain: Nicotinic receptors are LIGAND-GATED ION CHANNELS. When ACh binds, the receptor immediately opens, allowing Na⁺ in and K⁺ out. This produces a fast response (milliseconds).

wrongExplains.0: G-protein coupled describes MUSCARINIC receptors. Nicotinic receptors are ion channels.
wrongExplains.2: Enzyme-linked receptors are another category (like growth factor receptors). Nicotinic are ion channels.
wrongExplains.3: Nuclear receptors bind steroid hormones. Nicotinic receptors are membrane ion channels.

scaffolds.q: Muscarinic receptors are what type of receptor?
scaffolds.explain: Muscarinic receptors are G-PROTEIN COUPLED RECEPTORS (GPCRs). ACh binding activates intracellular signaling cascades. This takes longer (hundreds of milliseconds to seconds) but produces more varied effects.

wrongExplains.0: Ligand-gated ion channels describes NICOTINIC receptors. Muscarinic are GPCRs.
wrongExplains.2: Muscarinic receptors are membrane receptors, not cytoplasmic. They're embedded in the cell membrane.
wrongExplains.3: Enzyme-linked receptors are a different category. Muscarinic are specifically G-protein coupled.

scaffolds.q: Which receptor type is found at ALL autonomic ganglia (both sympathetic and parasympathetic)?
scaffolds.explain: NICOTINIC receptors are found at ALL autonomic ganglia. This is because ALL preganglionic neurons release ACh, and postganglionic neurons have nicotinic receptors to receive that signal quickly.

wrongExplains.0: Muscarinic receptors are at parasympathetic TARGET organs, not in the ganglia themselves.
wrongExplains.2: Beta-adrenergic receptors bind norepinephrine at sympathetic targets, not ACh at ganglia.
wrongExplains.3: Alpha-adrenergic receptors bind norepinephrine, not ACh. Ganglia use nicotinic ACh receptors.

scaffolds.q: Why are nicotinic receptors ideal for ganglionic transmission?
options: They produce fast responses because they're ion channels
scaffolds.explain: Ganglia need FAST transmission to relay signals quickly. Nicotinic receptors are ion channels that open immediately when ACh binds, producing rapid depolarization in milliseconds.

wrongExplains.0: Slow effects would delay autonomic responses. Nicotinic provides fast transmission.
wrongExplains.2: All receptors require their specific neurotransmitter (ACh for nicotinic) to be activated.
wrongExplains.3: Nicotinic receptors bind ACh, not norepinephrine. Adrenergic receptors bind NE.

scaffolds.q: Where are muscarinic receptors primarily found in the ANS?
options: At parasympathetic target organs (heart, glands, smooth muscle)
scaffolds.explain: Muscarinic receptors are at PARASYMPATHETIC TARGET ORGANS — the heart (M2), smooth muscle, and glands (M3). They receive ACh from parasympathetic postganglionic neurons.

wrongExplains.0: Ganglia use nicotinic receptors for fast transmission. Muscarinic are at target organs.
wrongExplains.2: Muscarinic receptors are in the brain AND at peripheral parasympathetic targets.
wrongExplains.3: Sympathetic postganglionic neurons mostly release NE onto adrenergic receptors, not ACh onto muscarinic.

scaffolds.q: The drug nicotine activates nicotinic receptors. What would happen if nicotine were given?
options: Both sympathetic and parasympathetic ganglia would be stimulated
scaffolds.explain: Nicotine activates nicotinic receptors at ALL ganglia — both sympathetic and parasympathetic. This is why nicotine causes mixed effects (increased heart rate but also gut stimulation).

wrongExplains.0: Nicotinic receptors are at BOTH types of ganglia, so both divisions would be affected.
wrongExplains.2: Both divisions use nicotinic receptors at their ganglia, so both would be stimulated.
wrongExplains.3: Nicotine definitely has autonomic effects — it acts at all autonomic ganglia.

scaffolds.q: A drug that blocks nicotinic receptors (ganglionic blocker) would affect ___.
scaffolds.explain: A ganglionic blocker affects BOTH divisions because both use nicotinic receptors at their ganglia. This produces widespread autonomic effects — often side effects limit clinical use.

wrongExplains.0: Nicotinic receptors are at BOTH types of ganglia, so blocking affects both divisions.
wrongExplains.1: Nicotinic receptors are at BOTH types of ganglia, so blocking affects both divisions.
wrongExplains.3: Both divisions rely on nicotinic receptors for ganglionic transmission, so blocking them has major effects.

scaffolds.q: Summary: Nicotinic receptors are at ganglia; muscarinic receptors are at ___.
scaffolds.explain: NICOTINIC = ganglia (both divisions). MUSCARINIC = parasympathetic target organs (heart, smooth muscle, glands). This separation allows selective drug targeting.

wrongExplains.0: Muscarinic receptors are at target organs, not ganglia. Ganglia use nicotinic.
wrongExplains.2: Skeletal muscle has nicotinic receptors (neuromuscular junction), not muscarinic.
wrongExplains.3: Muscarinic receptors are in the brain AND at peripheral parasympathetic targets.
